data.title: Private Credit Is Cracking: Are Public Equities Next?
data.description:
Over the last year, private credit has started to show several signs of stress, which made me ask the one question that matters (to investors in public equities): can a private credit blowout spill into public equities?

My answer is that it can, but the extent is quite limited. Right now, the damage seems to be contained in the private credit world. And, as I discuss in this article, I don’t see strong signs of any forced deleveraging that could mechanically tank the entire equity market. So far, the pessimism surrounding private credit is dragging down the main publicly traded alternative credit managers, but I don't see a spillover into other sectors like tech.

That said, since July 2024, there have been multiple sponsor-to-lender handovers, restructurings, and bankruptcies across very different assets, alongside renewed criticism that private marks can lag reality. In this article, I review the different events that led to rising pessimism in the private credit market. Buffett calls these factors cockroaches, so I’ll use a similar term below.

A Review Of The Cockroaches In Private Credit

There are a few patterns that, taken together, indicate a deterioration in the US private credit market.

The first cockroach in the kitchen was spotted on July 2, 2024, when Vista Equity was reportedly in talks to give up control of its company, Pluralsight, to private-credit lenders via a debt-to-equity conversion.

In my view, this marked the first major debt restructuring of a company that had borrowed from "shadow banks". To keep the terminology clear, on Wall Street, shadow banks are commonly known as lenders that don’t face the same regulatory obligations as traditional banks. More often than not, less regulation means more risks, and this is exactly what happened with Pluralsight. The red flag in the Vista story was the markdown to zero of a $1.6 billion equity investment in Pluralsight just a few months earlier (according to Reuters). Among the lenders that took control over the company, we have Blue Owl Capital, Ares Management, Golub Capital, Oaktree, Benefit Street Partners, Goldman Sachs, and BlackRock. After the restructuring was completed, a big chunk of debt ($1.2B according to some sources) was converted to equity.

As per Buffett , "there is seldom just one cockroach in the kitchen". Since the Pluralsight restructuring, I count 5 more. I discuss them briefly below.

In January 2025, Alacrity Solutions (owned by BlackRock) went through a similar restructuring where the sponsor relinquished control and direct lenders took ownership. Among those lenders, we have some familiar names again: Blue Owl, KKR, and Goldman Sachs AM. Roughly $1B senior and $0.5B junior debt converted into a $450M term loan plus about $250M preferred equity.

On February 6, 2025, Zips Car Wash (owned by Atlantic Street Capital) filed for Chapter 11. The company was seeking to cut $279M in debt and hand the keys to its lenders. Among the reasons for the bankruptcy, Zips cited rising interest rates, higher labor costs, and intensifying competition (all of which make sense to me, considering the tough business the company was operating in). The issue, however, was pointed out by Bloomberg, citing that loans were marked in the low-$0.90s relatively shortly before bankruptcy.

In other words, no one was able to see the train coming, and this fueled criticism that private marks can lag reality. Interestingly enough, the same criticism was made regarding the Alacrity Solutions restructuring.

Moving on, International Data Group (backed by Blackstone) was the next domino on the line to fall. Yet again, direct lenders converted part (20%-25%) of their debt (estimated at around $800M) into equity in April 2025.

The next two bankruptcies should be familiar to most readers due to their size and the fact that they happened within a short timeframe (which led to a lot of attention in the media). I'm talking about First Brands Group and Tricolor.

First Brands Group filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in September, with some irregularities detailed on September 30. The company said that it found an apparent $2.3B hole tied to off-balance-sheet invoice financing. The concerns included whether invoices were factored multiple times and whether collateral was commingled. According to the last filing, excluding factoring liabilities, the company had $9.3B of debt obligations. Another concern was related to a spillover to funds and banks. Jefferies disclosed $715M in receivables exposure via its asset-management unit, and UBS cited over $500M exposure in affected funds. On top of that, there is a DOJ inquiry and an SEC probe of Jefferies’ dealings. This bankruptcy added fuel to the fire in the private credit markets, especially as the bankruptcy was (likely) due to opacity and complexity in credit products.

At the same time, Tricolor filed for Chapter 7 liquidation. The size of this liquidation includes over $1B in assets and over $1B liabilities, with about 25,000 creditors. The controversy here came from one of the lenders, Fifth Third, which warned of alleged external fraudulent activity tied to a $200M loan. As a consequence, Fifth Third expects a $170M–$200M impairment, and JPMorgan had nearly $200M exposure (according to Reuters sources). I argue this was the icing on the cake, as it was a direct hit to the confidence in parts of asset-backed lending (remember that Tricolor was in the auto loan / used-car dealer business), adjacent to private credit.

Finally, and I wouldn't consider this a cockroach, we have the Blue Owl saga. As you may know from my article on Blue Owl, the company proposed merging its non-traded BDC (OBDC II) into its publicly traded BDC (OBDC). The problem came from a timing perspective, as in November, OBDC’s stock price was trading approximately 20% below its NAV. Here is the trade that shrewd investors quickly noticed:

Given that OBDC II investors could normally redeem at or near NAV, and the fact that OBDC shares were available in the market at a 20% discount, a good trade would be to redeem OBDC II at NAV, then buy OBDC cheaper in the market. Naturally, this kind of arbitrage would create a quick exit from OBDC II. To prevent such an avalanche, the company decided to stop OBDC II redemptions until the merger closed (expected Q1 2026). As a consequence, the company terminated the proposed merger on November 19, 2025. However, management said that they are planning to turn the tender window back on again in early 2026, so OBDC II investors would once again have a formal way to request cash out. I recommend keeping a close eye on any developments in the Blue Owl OBDC saga, as the story may not be over.

Am I Sweating?

Not really, but I am indeed keeping a close eye on any new cockroaches surfacing in private credit.

In my view, we are looking at a contained pain, specific to this sector. In other words, I don't see the cockroaches moving from the kitchen into the bedroom (i.e., hyperscalers and AI-exposed companies). I believe the spillover to broad public equities is likely limited. The IMF estimates that the most common private credit investment vehicle is a closed-end, capital call structure with a limited life, meaning investors generally can’t demand cash tomorrow. This leads to fewer forced panic-selloffs (remember 2008).

Speaking of 2008, banks are better capitalized, and according to the last FEDS report, "the financial stability implications of banks' lending to private credit seem limited so far". Furthermore, the Fed’s Financial Stability Report (released back in April this year) describes the banking system as sound and resilient. Note on the "leverage in the financial sector" column below that broker leverage is low.

Furthermore, the Fed also noted a split between public and private firms, with weaker coverage ratios being more of an issue for private firms.

My final argument is the fact that private credit is just 9% of the total outstanding nonfinancial corporate debt. At the moment, most of the headwinds are contained

Looking Ahead

The chart below shows the 1-year performance of publicly traded alternative credit managers vs the S&P500.

Given the deterioration in sentiment due to the factors that I discussed in this article, all of them have underperformed the S&P500 and are down double digits over the last 12 months.

From a fundamentals perspective, all these alternative credit managers have experienced decent revenue growth over the last 3-5 years. Furthermore, the 1-year top-line projections suggest double-digit growth for all these players, with KKR and Blue Owl projected to grow 24% yoy next year.

Call me crazy, but I don't see a clear justification for the underperformance of these stocks over the last year, except for the deterioration in sentiment.

Looking ahead, I would keep a close eye on the Blue Owl OBDC saga, and I would closely monitor more bankruptcies in the private and public sectors. From what I'm seeing, most of the cockroaches spotted since mid-2024 are not suggesting systemic risks that could spill to public equities. Most of them are the result of tough business models (used car sales) and complex credit products. I just don't see enough leverage in the private credit markets and a clear catalyst ahead that would suggest a blowout.

data.title: SCHD ETF: 2025 Reconstitution Impact And 2026 Outlook
data.description:
SCHD ETF: energy exposure in focus

I last covered the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) on 10.21 with an article titled “SCHD ETF: REIT Dividends Too Attractive To Exclude”. That article was motivated by the ETF’s last dividend declaration and gave it a Hold rating. Since that analysis, there have been a few notable changes worthy of a reexamination. In the rest of this article, I will concentrate on the top 2 changes on my list: the fund’s latest exposure disclosure and also the macroeconomic parameters facing oil prices.

As a background, the SCHD has long been a popular foundational holding for many income-focused investors and the fund performs an but its annual reconstitution (typically in March of every year). The 2025 reconstitution saw a notable increase in the ETF's exposure to the Energy sector (more on this later). Due to such a shift, the energy sector is now the fund’s largest exposure, representing about 19.3% of its total assets per the fund’s more recent disclosure shown below. Next, I will explain why such a shift has been detrimental to its 2025 total return and could continue to be so in 2026 due to the ongoing oil price pressure.

SCHD: basic information and exposure

Let me take a quick digression and cover the basics of SCHD in case there are readers new to the fund. As indicated in its name, SCHD focuses on dividend paying stocks. Its stock selection methodology is centered on assessing dividend quality, which relies on a framework of four distinct financial metrics as depicted in the chart and quoted from its fund description below:

SCHD Fund Description: The Fund’s goal is to track as closely as possible, before fees and expenses, the total return of the Dow Jones U.S. Dividend 100™ Index. SCHD’s index focuses on the sustainability and quality of dividends by screening companies based on four dividend quality attributes. These screens have provided enhanced yield and quality factors to the portfolio, along with distinct sector exposures.

As a reflection (or a result) of its above approach, the fund’s holdings are heavily tilted towards more defensive sectors and stocks. The SCHD ETF holds a relatively small number of 102 stocks, with its Top 10 Holdings constituting a substantial 42.30% of the entire portfolio as you can see from the following table. The top holdings include several healthcare stocks with its largest single position being Merck & Co at 4.98%, followed closely by Amgen at 4.86%. Other top holdings include consumer staples (Coca-Cola Co at 4.24% and PepsiCo at 4.00%). Even though Chevron is the only energy stock in the top 10, the sector represents the fund’s largest exposure as aforementioned and I will elaborate on the implications immediately below.

SCHD ETF: 2025 annual reconstitution and 2026 outlook

The next two charts summarize the fund’s 2025 annual reconstitution performed in March. As seen in the first bar chart, the largest net additions are toward energy stocks, which saw an addition of 5 holdings. As seen in the second table below, these changes have resulted in a net exposure increase of 8.8% toward the energy sector, bringing the fund’s exposure to energy stocks to 21.0%. Due to the underperformance of energy stocks since then, the weight of these stocks has decreased to the 19.3% as aforementioned, implying a relative underperformance of around 9%.

Looking ahead, I am afraid SCHD’s heavy exposure to energy stocks will keep dragging its performance as I see persisting pressure on oil prices. Out of the many factors, three of them stand out in my mind. First, I expect OPEC+ countries to continue production at elevated levels for 2026 and likely for 2027 also (see this Seeking Alpha news for more background). Second, I see large odds for the Ukraine peace talks to lead to the end of this multi-year war, thus bringing back Russian, a key oil-exporting country, back to the global energy market.

The third and probably most crucial factor is China. Recent reports (e.g., see this Wall Street Journal report and also the EIT estimates quoted below) show that China kept filling its strategic petroleum reserves. The sheer magnitude of China’s demand translates into a strong supporting force for oil prices. This could be double-edged sword because oil prices could drop considerably if the country’s demand softens. As an example, the Wall Street Journal report mentioned above projects that “If China really stops buying, the path toward low $50 would be very quick”.

EIA estimate (Oct 9, 2025): Crude oil inventories in China increased by about 900,000 barrels per day (b/d) between January and August this year, essentially acting as a source of demand by removing barrels from the global markets. The stock builds in China limited the downward price pressure we would otherwise expect to see with growing inventories, keeping the Brent crude oil spot prices in a relatively tight range around $68 per barrel (B) in the second and third quarters of 2025.

And I do see signs that could indicate softening demand from China. As an example, the table below displays data provided by the Center on Global Energy Policy. My takeaway from the statistics here are mainly twofold. Firstly, China’s demand for oil has only increased very slightly – if at all - in 2024. Industry estimates of the growth rate varied from a range of 2% to -2%. Second and more importantly, projections for 2025 point to continued growth uncertainties from these same sources. Two of the sources suggest 1% of growth only, and the other two suggest growth in the 2~3% range.

Other risks and final thoughts

As another indicator for China’s softening energy demand, the country’s real estate sector has been in decline for years and show no sign of recovery in my view. To wit, the floor space of new home starts in China decreased by more than 2/3 between 2019 and 2024 (see the next bar chart). Such a sharp decline can impact oil demand directly and also indirectly. Construction equipment runs on diesel fuel, which is made from oil. A slowing real-estate sector can also decrease the demand from transportation and other related services.

To keep the analysis balanced, let me wrap up by pointing out a few positives. As a shareholder of SCHD myself, I can testify that the fund’s popularity is for good reasons. The top considerations that went into my own decision include its sound screening method as quoted earlier, low fees, sizable AUM, and also low trading friction. More importantly, the fund is also priced quite attractively in an otherwise expansive equity market. Judging by its yield, which hovers around 3.76% as of this writing (shown by the solid red line in the chart below), it is not only far above the average in the past 10 years (of around 3.26% as shown by the grey dashed line) but also surpassed the +1 standard deviation level (shown by the orange line with round symbols), indicating unusually attractive valuation. In terms of P/E, the fund trades at a multiple of around 17.5x. With a 5-year earnings growth rate of 10.84% and a current yield of 3.76%, the PEGY (P/E growth yield) ratio is around 1.2x, quite close to the golden standard of 1x.

To close, past experiences have taught me successful investment starts with a clear understandings – and also delineation - of one’s time horizon. That is why in our Investing Group Service (Envision Early Retirement) monitors both short- and long-term catalysts. In the case of SCHD, I’m not seeing an obvious alpha in the short run (say in 2026 or even 2027) given its large exposure to energy stocks and the ongoing headwinds facing the sector. For investors oriented towards the long run (say 5 years or more), the combination of attractive valuation, generous yield, and sound investing approach could deliver outsized total returns, especially on a risk-adjusted basis.

data.title: Caterpillar: AI Tailwinds Are Real, Yet The Core Business Keeps Me Neutral
data.description:
Caterpillar (CAT) is an American engineering equipment heavyweight, whose stock price surged by 41%, mainly driven by AI-powered tailwinds tied to electricity demand and the need for generators and energy-storage solutions. Although it captures a bullish narrative, the legacy business drags the outlook, and I think the market may have run ahead of fundamentals too far and too quickly.

The company trades at a forward P/E of 31x, representing a 51% premium versus the sector median of 20x. In addition to this, CAT trades at nearly a 35% premium versus the average company in the S&P500 at a 23x earnings multiple. Over the past 12 months, CAT experienced severe top and bottom-line headwinds, which resulted in contracted business performance, yet it trades at a premium to the benchmark. This makes me feel cautious.

I rate Caterpillar a Hold with a $600 price target over the next year. This presents a 5% upside possibility, but unlikely to outperform the benchmark. I wouldn't be surprised if CAT consolidated after a significant rally, as fundamentals may need to catch up to the expectations.

FQ3 2025: Resumed Top-Line Growth Appears Promising

On October 29, the construction company delivered FQ3 2025 results. This was a strong quarter for the company, indicating resumed revenue growth after six consecutive contraction in top-line performance. The market reacted to the results with a 12% upside on the earnings day.

Caterpillar posted $17.64 billion in revenue, reflecting a 9.51% growth on a year-over-year basis, and $864 million above what the market had anticipated. The bottom-line arrived at $4.95 in diluted EPS, an approximately 4% decline versus $5.17 in the same quarter last year, but $0.43 above analysts' estimates. The turnaround story appears to be in play, and double-beat quarterly performance justifies that.

In the earnings presentation, management has underlined that it generated $3.1 billion in operating profit, indicating a 3% decline on a year-over-year. However, CAT also highlighted that it has experienced a $2.4 billion increase in order backlog versus the FQ2 2024 estimate, signaling an all-time high figure, reaching $39.8 billion. This supports a bullish outlook and upward trend, mainly influenced by rising demand in the energy and transportation segment.

The construction business delivered $6.8 billion in revenue, a 7% growth on a year-over-year basis, but experienced a drop in profit due to unfavorable price realization and a rise in manufacturing costs, influenced by tariffs. The segment profit dropped from $1.49 billion to $1.38 billion, a 7% contraction over the past 12 months.

I think this remains concerning and drags the outlook. Investors should monitor whether margins contract further, as this could indicate a more structural issue. The construction industry remains the second-largest segment, and this may have a significant impact on the top and bottom-line performance over the following quarters.

The resources industries achieved $3.1 billion in sales, a 2% growth on a year-over-year basis. This heavily contributes to my neutral stance, indicating that legacy business appears to be dragging and offsetting the bullish narrative. In addition to this, due to concerns tied to tariffs, the segment experienced a 19% drop in profit, reflecting a margin drop from 20.3% to 16%. Although top-line appears to be running well on all cylinders, tariffs weigh on the company's profitability, and I think that may cap the upside.

The energy and transportation segment, tailored with AI-powered products and demand, has represented the most rapid top-line growth. The business posted $8.4 billion in total sales, underscoring a 17% surge on a year-over-year basis. This appears to be promising and offsets the legacy business's bearish performance. In addition to this, the segment achieved a 17% surge in profit, yet margins remained roughly flat at 20%. This underscores business stability, but it is also pressured due to tariffs.

The financial product segment delivered $1.08 billion in revenue, a 4% growth on a year-over-year basis, but about a 3% decline in profit, marking $241 million. This presents a mixed broader picture for the industrials business.

Overall, the AI-powered tailwinds remain intact. The company is experiencing significant growth in the energy and transport space, mainly driven by energy-storage and power infrastructure demand. However, the legacy business weighs on the future outlook, and I remain cautious. The company anticipates margin expansion over the following quarters, but headwinds tied to tariffs may cap the outlook. Caterpillar remains a solid business with a significant moat, but the market may have run ahead of the company's fundamentals.

Premium Valuation Disregards Legacy Business Drag

Caterpillar trades at a forward P/E of approximately 31, reflecting a 51% premium versus the sector median of 20x earnings multiple. This remains significant, supported by a multi-year high valuation, represented by the chart below. Although the company may continue trading at such a premium, I believe the upward move appears overextended, and there is risk for consolidation.

On a forward PEG (non-GAAP) of 4.03, the company appears to be roughly 2.2x overvalued versus peers at 1.83. This is significant and supports my consolidation thesis. The forward EV/Sales and Price/Sales ratios of 4.58 and 4.06, respectively, suggest a 2.2x premium at the midpoint versus sector medians of 2.16 and 1.74. Based on the overvaluation, I argue that the market may have already priced in the future revenue growth, and it leaves little room for error for management if demand weakens.

The industrials giant has a heavily leveraged capital structure, representing $41.53 billion in total obligations. The company has $6.63 billion in cash, which partially offsets the debt, but leverage remains. This is another argument supporting a more neutral stance. Caterpillar paid $133 million in interest expense last quarter to service the debt, and that weighs on profitability.

Although the company's top-line performance accelerated, on a TTM basis, CAT's revenue declined by 2%. This represents meaningful underperformance versus peers 4% top-line growth. In addition to this, the bottom line contracted by 10% versus peers 6% growth. Although I understand the markets are forward-looking, the current valuation premium versus the benchmark indicates a 35% gap appears to be extended. Of course, the industrial company may continue trading at such a valuation further, but investors should remain cautious.

The company's profitability metrics also highlight a mixed picture. The gross profit margin (TTM) of 30% suggests a 4% underperformance versus peers at 31.42%. However, the net income margin (TTM) of 14% indicates a nearly 2.3x outperformance versus the sector median of 6%. This argues for a premium valuation, but I am not sure whether the current disconnect is justified.

Management achieved a 12% return on total capital (TTM), implying a 74% advantage versus peers at 7%. The industrials company generated $11.54 billion in cash from operations (TTM), a substantial outperformance versus peers at $372 million.

To conclude, Caterpillar remains a significant player in the industrials space. The company appears to be successfully riding the AI-tailored wave due to electricity infrastructure demand, but the legacy business is clouding the outlook. The broader picture remains mixed, yet the stock trades at a massive premium. CAT is heavily leveraged with a significant financial burden, and that is an additional argument for my neutral stance.

Wall Street analysts forecast $18.64 in diluted EPS for FY2025 and $22.23 in diluted EPS for FY2026. I generally agree with the expectations if AI-driven demand continues, and CAT maintains elevated margins. However, I think the company may consolidate closer to market-like valuation, as the current upward rally appears overdone. If we apply a 27x earnings multiple, representing a 12% contraction versus the current of about 31x, but still elevated versus the benchmark's at 23x, to the FY2027 EPS estimate of $22.23, we arrive at my price target of $600. I think this appears to be a reasonable estimate, but it will be interesting to see whether projections materialize.

Main Risks And Concerns

Caterpillar is tied to risks and concerns that could meaningfully impact the stock price performance in the short term.

The recent rally appears to be boosted by AI-driven demand, and if it weakens, the stock may correct from premium levels. In addition to this, the company's valuation nearly tripled over the past years, and it may be argued that this catalyst is priced in to a large extent. I think the stock may be due for consolidation, but if AI demand weakens, it may meaningfully correct.

The industrial company's legacy business delivers a single-digit revenue growth. Given the fact that the company trades at a significant premium, the market appears to be discounting any headwinds tied to a significant portion of the CAT's business. There are tariff concerns, which are weighing on profitability, signaling a more structural issue the company may be facing. If the trend continues, it may be challenging for investors to justify a premium earnings multiple.

Caterpillar's capital structure remains heavily leveraged. The machinery company is paying a substantial interest expense to service its debt, and it clouds the bottom-line expansion. If the Fed decides to maintain elevated funding rates further, it could cloud the bullish outlook.

On the other hand, if AI-powered tailwinds materialize, the stock may deliver double-digit bottom-line growth, fueling the stock price higher.

A Significant Market Dominance, But Headwinds Are Real, Too

I think Caterpillar remains an interesting diversification to long-term investors, lacking industrial exposure. The company has a significant presence in the space, operates at appealing margins, and continues steady revenue performance despite the tariffs. It also unlocked a new growth cycle tied to AI-driven demand for electricity and its infrastructure, which could boost the stock price higher if it materializes.

However, based on my analysis, it seems that the market may feel too optimistic about CAT's future growth. The stock is trading at a premium versus both its peers and the benchmark, and it may be challenging to justify significant expansion from these levels. CAT remains highly leveraged, and legacy business drags the future outlook. If the trend continues, investors may feel a bit more cautious, capping the upside and consolidating.

I rate Caterpillar a Hold with a $600 price target over the next year. This presents a 5% upside possibility, but I don't think it is likely to outperform the broader market. 28 Wall Street analysts are indicating a roughly $588 price target over the next year, implying a 2% upside possibility. This comes approximately in line with my expectations, but it will be interesting to see whether forecasts materialize.

data.title: New Preferred Stock And Baby Bond IPOs, November 2025
data.description:
New offering summaries:

Following a similar preferred stock playbook recently used by Strategy (MSTR), the company Strive (ASST) which characterizes itself as a “Bitcoin treasury company,” priced an offering of $160 million worth of new series A perpetual preferred stock at $80/share. The new shares have a liquidation preference of $100, against which the company advertised a floating rate dividend, to be set at an initial 12%. However, the actual future dividend rate is flexible, with adjustments at the company’s discretion, within a set of published guidelines. The company stated that its current intention – which it qualified is subject to change at its discretion – would be to tweak the dividend rate so that it nudges the trading price into a long-term range between $95 and $105 per share. At its option, the company may redeem the shares at any time, for $110 each. The new shares traded temporarily on the OTC under symbol STIVV, before moving to permanent symbol SATA on the Nasdaq.

Alpine Income Property Trust (PINE) priced an offering of $50 million worth of new series A cumulative perpetual preferred stock, offering a fixed dividend rate of 8%. Call protection is available for a full five years, with the first callable date in November of 2030. The company indicated that offering proceeds will be used in part towards property acquisitions, commercial loan and investment opportunities and repayment of debt. The new shares are unrated by a credit ratings agency, but rank senior to the common stock traded under symbol PINE which presently has a market capitalization north of $200 million. Trading took place temporarily on the OTC under symbol APNEV before moving to permanent symbol PINE-A on the New York Stock Exchange.

Global Medical REIT Inc. (GMRE) priced an offering of $50 million worth of new series B cumulative perpetual preferred stock, offering a fixed dividend rate of 8%. The shares become callable by the company in five years, with the first callable date in November of 2030. The common shares presently trade with a market capitalization north of $400 million, providing a level of “cushion” to the new preferred shares which are not being rated by a credit ratings agency. The company indicated that proceeds from the offering may be used for purposes including new acquisitions and repayment of debt. The new preferred shares traded temporarily on the OTC under symbol GMRBV before moving to permanent symbol GMRE-B on the New York Stock Exchange.

M&T Bank Corporation (MTB) priced an offering of $450 million worth of new series K non-cumulative perpetual preferred stock, offering a fixed dividend rate of 6.35%. The new shares received credit ratings from all four of Moody’s, S&P, Fitch, and DBRS, at Baa3, BB+, BBB- and BBB respectively. The new shares are trading on the New York Stock Exchange under symbol MTB-K.

Financial services company Bread Financial Holdings, Inc. (BFH) priced an offering of $75 million worth of new series A non-cumulative perpetual preferred stock, offering a fixed dividend rate of 8.625%. The company indicated that a portion of the proceeds could be lent to one of its subsidiary banks, Comenity Capital Bank, and also potentially used for common share repurchases. The new shares were rated in “speculative” territory by both Moody’s and Fitch, receiving credit ratings of B1 and B- respectively. Trading is taking place temporarily on the OTC under symbol BFHIV, until the shares move to permanent symbol BFH-A on the New York Stock Exchange.

And finally Redwood Trust, Inc. (RWT) priced an offering of $100 million worth of new exchange traded senior notes due 2030, offering a fixed coupon of 9.5%. No credit rating was noted in the prospectus documents filed with the SEC, but earlier in the year the company issued $90 million worth of exchange traded notes due 2030 with a 9.125% coupon (RWTP) and those notes had been rated BBB- by Egan-Jones Ratings Company. The new 9.5% notes trade on the New York Stock Exchange under symbol RWTQ.

SEC filings for further information: SATA, PINE-A, GMRE-B, MTB-K, BFH-A, RWTQ

Buying new shares for wholesale

Preferred stock IPOs often involve a temporary period during which OTC trading symbols are assigned until these securities move to their retail exchange, at which time they will receive their permanent symbols. For example the new Global Medical REIT preferred discussed above began trading on the OTC earlier this month under symbol GMRBV:

Individual investors, armed with a web browser and an online trading account, can often purchase newly introduced preferred stock shares at wholesale prices just like the big guys (see "Preferred Stock Buyers Change Tactics For Double-Digit Returns" for an explanation of how the OTC can be used to purchase shares for discounted prices).

Those who have been following this strategy of using the wholesale OTC exchange to buy newly introduced shares as close to $25 (or less) as possible are more able to avoid a capital loss if prices drop (if they choose to sell). Note that the above Global Medical example is now trading above par value as of this writing.

Your broker will automatically update the trading symbols of any shares you purchase on the OTC, once they move to their permanent symbols. A special note regarding preferred stock trading symbols: Annoyingly, unlike common stock trading symbols, the format used by exchanges, brokers and other online quoting services for preferred stock symbols is not standardized.

For example, a given Series A preferred stock might have a symbol ending in “-A” at TDAmeritrade, Google Finance and several others but this same security may end in “PR.A” at E*Trade and “.PA” at Seeking Alpha. For a cross-reference table of how preferred stock symbols are denoted by sixteen popular brokers and other online quoting services, see “Preferred Stock Trading Symbol Cross-Reference Table.”

Past preferred stock IPOs below par

In addition to covering new preferred stock and ETD offerings, here at Preferred Stock Alerts we also track past offerings, with alerts when securities fall below their par values. Year-to-date, our “bargain table” count of the highest quality preferred stocks and ETDs available at a discount to par value, is down from the April peak but remains elevated by historical standards, at a current count of 148, and our in-house “Perfect Market Index” is also down from April highs but still flashes “buyer’s market” as its current reading:

We track when different preferred stocks and exchange traded debt securities become available below par in market trading, and we would like to share with you some of the most recent dips/crosses below par we have observed among individual securities (note that yellow highlighted entries indicate highly rated securities eligible for our “Bargain Table”):

For those preferred stock (and ETD) investors interested not just in new IPOs but previously issued securities, following par crosses can provide useful insight into which securities have recently become available in the marketplace below their initial offering prices.

Preferred stock market metrics

Since our last article, the average market prices of Compliance-Score-10 preferred stocks (and ETDs) have continued to fluctuate around the $24.50 level (a current discount to par of about 2% and representing a current yield of about 6.53%). Here is how the main metrics we track currently look over the past month:

Investor Takeaway

In our monthly Seeking Alpha articles, we here at Preferred Stock Alerts typically summarize all the new preferred stock and exchange traded debt offerings observed over the course of the month; we also highlight past offerings that have interesting alert activity on our dashboard. Our goal is to keep fixed-income investors up-to-date on the various investments available in the current marketplace, and we hope this month’s article has served this purpose. See you next time, and thanks for reading!

data.title: One Quarter Was Enough To Send 1stdibs.Com From Cheap To Expensive (Downgrade)
data.description:
1stDibs (DIBS) reported 3Q25 results, surprising the market very positively with an improvement in profitability, and opening the way for potential adjusted EBITDA profitability in 4Q25 and FY26.

As speculated in my original article on DIBS, from September 2025, the optionality embedded in the stock was triggered by one set of good results. When I wrote about DIBS, the name was basically trading for its cash holdings and little more, meaning any expectation of future profitability would trigger a price improvement. Indeed, the stock has appreciated more than 100% since.

Despite the good results and the potential for DIBS to eventually reach adjusted EBITDA profitability and even operational profitability, I believe the stock has lost its appeal. The name no longer offers a price similar to net cash holdings, and the promise of profitability is now more expensive. The stock no longer conserves the same upside optionality and has added downside risk if results take longer to materialize than expected.

For that reason, and despite the good results, I move the stock to Hold.

A good quarter was enough

In my original article on DIBS, I had written that "I believe the chances that the company suddenly turns a profit are low, but the market already discounts that, because the company basically trades at little above liquidation value."

The results from this quarter do not modify my read that the chances that the company turns a profit are low, but have significantly increased the market's odds of such a profit becoming a reality.

In one sentence, DIBS reduced its adjusted EBITDA margin from -14% a year ago to -1% this quarter. The company also guided for an adjusted EBITDA positive margin in 4Q25 (potentially the first time since IPO) and positive FCF in FY26.

Why did this happen? A mix of price increases for the company's services, plus a reduction in performance marketing expenses and sales headcount.

Starting with revenues, they increased 4% YoY. This happens despite (or potentially even because of) the seller count falling 17% YoY (but stable sequentially since 4Q24).

The reason is that the company discontinued an option to list items without paying for a monthly subscription, but paying a higher commission when the object was sold. DIBS considered that this attracted too many low-value sellers, which added to overhead without contributing value. We have to remember that DIBS manages a high-trust marketplace (value of items is in the thousands), where sellers need to be closely monitored. Adding sellers is not as simple as creating an account, because it increases these monitoring costs.

The result of churning off these low-value sellers is that the company did not lose GMV (up 5% YoY) nor listings (up 1% YoY), and was able to extract a little more revenue from the tail end of customers (those sellers that decide to continue listing by upgrading to a subscription).

The impact of the new policy was also felt in S&M. The company was able to reduce its headcount and its performance marketing expenses, which were allocated to these low-value users. In total, the company saved $2 million in S&M (a 22% reduction), offset by severance (non-recurring) of $0.7 million, leading to a 13% reported reduction (details on the 10-Q's MD&A).

The company said on the call that yearly savings reach $7 million in total.

These developments do strengthen my view expressed in the original article that the company has moat characteristics. It can increase effective prices and reduce marketing expenses without losing revenues. Management clarified during the call that 75% of its traffic is organic, and that its Seller Survey indicates DIBS is the first sales channel for the majority of sellers, even above the sellers' showrooms.

Potentially, with the help of higher activity (holidays) and the lower impact of severance versus savings from lower headcount, the company guided for positive adjusted EBITDA in 4Q25 (2/5% margin) and for positive FCF in FY26.

These two datapoints (good 3Q25, better expectations ahead), plus the announcement of a $12 million repurchase authorization, probably led to the explosive appreciation of the company's stock since October. Why the rally started in mid-October, when results were released in early November, is unclear.

Valuation is now less attractive

When I issued a Buy on DIBS in September, the name traded at about $100 million. Back then, I wrote that: "If we discount the net liabilities from cash, we are considering $65 million in net cash reserves, which is approximately 63% of the company's market cap today ($102 million). Cash losses, as seen above, are about $3 million per year, so that, if the company maintains the current level of operations, it should not lose much cash. However, as long as the company maintains SBC of $15 million, the sharecount will increase by about 5 million shares per year (12% from a fully-diluted standpoint) at current prices."

That is, the company traded for 1.5x net cash, losing very little of that cash per year, while diluting at about 10/15% yearly. This was the 'business as usual scenario', already discounted. The positive, profitable company scenario needed to be added on top of the net cash, and therefore the stock offered optionality.

However, at the time, I also considered that profitability was not that easy to achieve. I wrote that: "Assuming 72% gross margins, and OpEx of about $85 million, plus a combined take rate of 25% (sales take rate plus fees), the company would need to grow its GMV to $472 million (30% from the current $360 million TTM) in order to reach operational breakeven."

These figures remain mostly valid today. Assuming $80 million in OpEx instead of $85 million (annualizing from 3Q25 without considering severance), and maintaining the take rate and gross margin, the company's GMV should reach $444 million per year ($80 million divided by 0.72, divided by 0.25) to achieve operational breakeven.

Based on current results and guidance, GMV should be flat for the year at $362 million, implying a 22% increase is needed to reach operational breakeven. The company has unfortunately not provided evidence of growth yet, as most of the improvement came from expenses, not growth in GMV (which has been flat since 2023 at least, and potentially since 2020, according to the quarterly presentation, page 12).

Furthermore, that 22% increase, which also assumes full operational leverage of OpEX, does not generate any profits, only breakeven.

Today, the company trades at almost $210 million, which, after removing the net cash (which remains around $65 million), leads to a value for equity net of cash of $145 million.

If we were to ask for a 5% earnings yield, an adjusted P/E of 20x (which is high, but suffices for our example), then the company should generate $7.25 million in net income, or almost $10 million pre-tax, increasing the GMV figure to $500 million, or almost 38% above current levels. This is assuming full operational leverage (zero increase in OpEx despite a large increase in business activity), and what could be considered a high multiple (20x) on already adjusted P (because we are netting cash).

Overall, I think the name is now optimistically priced and has therefore lost its upside optionality. For that reason, I moved the name to a Hold rating.

The upside risk of the thesis is that DIBS continues to show increasingly better results, adding to the optimism embedded in the stock price. The downside risk is that the company's GMV continues to stagnate, and that the market reduces its confidence in future profitability, re-discounting the name.

data.title: Best AI-Resistant Idea Competition: The Winners
data.description:
In October, Seeking Alpha invited analysts to participate in a Best AI-Resistant Idea competition. We received 26 submissions. Here's a look at all the winners as determined by the judging editors:

1) Apollonian Research – OneMain Holdings: An AI-Resilient 7% Yield Structured To Manage Credit Stress - OMF has stable management, safe dividends, transparent reporting, decent valuation, and a proven capacity to weather bad markets; it looks attractive partially because of the perceived competition by a host of FinTech companies, and with possible stress in the non/near-prime credit market that would be disproportionately negative for its competitors, this looks like a great setup for OMF.

2) Banana Stand Capital – GitLab: AI Is A Catalyst, Not A Threat - GTLB is undervalued amid AI disruption fears, but its security and reliability focus position it to benefit from rising AI-generated code; trading at 6x EV/Sales with 29% revenue growth, GTLB offers attractive upside and is a strong buy, further supported by takeover potential and robust free cash flow margins.

3) Rational Expectations – Upwork Likely To See Secular Growth At Attractive Valuation - UPWK has recently optimized for profitability, delivering high returns on capital and robust gross margins, and now trades at an undemanding valuation near 10x earnings; the business is resilient to AI disruption, adapting to new demand trends and leveraging AI to enhance platform efficiency and expand service offerings.

We selected the winners based on how compelling the thesis is (50%) and independent insights (50%).

First place receives an award of US $1,500, second place US $1,250, and third place US $1,000.

Thank you to everyone who participated - we certainly enjoyed reading your submissions. For those who didn't place, don't worry, we’ll post another competition in the near future. Stay tuned for more details.

data.title: Alibaba: Take Advantage Of The Current Setback
data.description:
In early September 2025, I wrote my last article about Alibaba Group Holding Limited (BABA) and although I did not rate the stock as a “Strong Buy” anymore, I remained absolutely bullish about the stock. In the conclusion of my last article, I argued that despite some risks, the stock remained deeply undervalued:

I remain bullish about Alibaba and still see the company as a "Buy". Although I still see Alibaba as being deeply undervalued, I would not see it as a "Strong Buy" at this point, as there are risks surrounding the business (for example, not being able to purchase NVIDIA H20 chips, which could be an opportunity as well as a problem) or the trade war escalating again, which will have a negative impact on the business (and stock price). But I remain bullish and continue to hold my Alibaba shares.

In the weeks following the article, the stock increased from $145 to its 52-week high of $192. However, following that rally, the stock entered a correction and declined to about $160 again. Nevertheless, the stock still increased 8% in value and let’s make no mistake – an increase of 8% in less than three months is a great performance and results in an annualized return of almost 40%.

Following several bullish articles about Alibaba, I will once again argue that the third-biggest Chinese company ( by market capitalization) is still one of the best investments we can make for the long term. I will argue that Alibaba is still trading for rather low valuation multiples (at least when taking the growth potential into account) and that Alibaba has several ways to grow its business in the years to come and we will start by looking at the last quarterly results.

Quarterly Results

On November 25, 2025, Alibaba reported second quarter results for fiscal 2026 and revenue increased from RMB 236,503 million in Q2/25 to RMB 247,795 million in Q2/26 – resulting in 4.8% year-over-year growth. But while revenue increased, operating income declined steeply from RMB 35,246 million in the same quarter last year to RMB 5,365 million in this quarter – resulting in a decline of 84.8%. Income from operations declined mostly due to investments in quick commerce, user experience, and technology. Diluted earnings per share also declined 52.0% year-over-year from RMB 2.27 in Q2/25 to RMB 1.09 in Q2/26.

Free cash flow switched from a positive amount of RMB 13,735 million in the same quarter last year to a negative amount of RMB 21,840 million in this quarter. The decrease in free cash flow was mainly attributed to the investments in quick commerce as well as cloud infrastructure.

Alibaba China E-commerce Group

This segment remains the most important segment for Alibaba as it is generating the biggest part of revenue. In Q2/26, revenue was RMB 132,578 million and the segment reported 15.5% year-over-year growth. Revenue growth was especially driven by the Quick Commerce Business, which reported revenue growth of 60% - mainly due to order growth as a result of the rollout of “Taobao Instant Commerce”, which is another layer on top of the traditional e-commerce marketplace of Alibaba. The service is integrating restaurants, supermarkets, convenience stores, or pharmacies, and promises a one-hour delivery for a broad range of items.

The number of 88VIP members, the group of customers spending the most money, continued to grow in the double digits year-over-year and surpassed 56 million and Alibaba will continue to focus on improving the retention of 88VIP memberships by offering these customers additional value propositions.

Alibaba International Digital Commerce Group

The Alibaba International Digital Commerce Group also reported solid growth rates and revenue increased 9.9% year-over-year to RMB 34,799 million while adjusted EBITDA switched from a negative amount of RMB 2,905 million in the same quarter last year to RMB 162 million.

Cloud Intelligence Group

Finally, the Cloud Intelligence Group was growing at a very high pace and revenue jumped from RMB 29,610 million in Q2/25 to RMB 39,824 million in Q2/26 – resulting in 34.5% year-over-year growth. Adjusted EBITDA also improved from RMB 2,661 million in the same quarter last year to RMB 3,604 million in this quarter – an increase of 35.4% year-over-year. The growth momentum was primarily driven by public cloud revenue growth, including the increasing adoption of AI-related products.

While the Cloud Intelligence Group already grew with a high pace, revenue from AI-related products achieved triple-digit year-over-year growth for the ninth consecutive quarter. In September, Alibaba introduced several new AI tools at the Apsara Conference. Innovations included new AI foundation models, services for model training or the Platform for AI (PAI). Alibaba is also contributing actively to open-source community development and the company has more than 180,000 derivative models based on the Qwen family on Hugging Face.

Growth Potential

When looking at the three different segments, Alibaba has without much doubt huge growth potential. The company is accelerating the growth in its cloud and AI business and for the core business – the e-commerce business – we can also expect high growth rates in the next few years. Mordor Intelligence is expecting growth rates slightly above 10% in the next few years until fiscal 2030 and Grand View Research is expecting even growth rates as high as 16% in the same timeframe.

While these are certainly high growth expectations, we also see growth rates slowing down in China. For the most recent Singles Day, transactions rose 17.5% year-over-year to nearly 1.7 trillion yuan (about $240 billion). Without much doubt, this is a high growth rate, but in the previous year growth was still 26.6% and this year’s shopping festival ran longer than ever. We don’t have precise numbers for Alibaba’s Singles Day, but management stated during the last earnings call that growth rates were in the double-digits.

Alibaba is also trying to expand to new markets, which includes its international expansion as well as offering new products and services. The international expansion is especially focused on the Southeast Asia region, but with AliExpress the company is also trying to conquer European countries. And in theory, this offers huge growth potential for Alibaba.

Recently, it was announced that Alibaba started selling its Quark AI smart glasses, which are powered by the Qwen and Quark’s multimodal AI model. The Quark S1 is selling for a start price of 3,799 yuan (about $540) and the Quark 1 model is available for half that price. It remains to be seen how this strategy will work out for Alibaba and it seems like the Chinese company is following a similar path to Meta Platforms. Right now, these smart glasses are available online and in over 600 stores and for 2026, Alibaba is planning an international version.

At this point, analysts are actually quite optimistic about revenue growth in the years to come. Between fiscal 2025 and fiscal 2035, analysts are estimating that revenue will grow at a CAGR of 10.55%.

Aside from revenue growing with a high pace, Alibaba might also be able to improve its margins again in the years to come. Of course, with the heavy investments Alibaba is making right now, we can assume that margins will continue to be low in the coming quarters. But over the long run, margins will probably improve again.

Additionally, Alibaba can continue growing its bottom line through share buybacks. Since 2021, Alibaba decreased the number of shares outstanding from 2,755 million to 2,396 million – resulting in a CAGR close to 3%. During the last quarter, Alibaba repurchased a total of 17 million ordinary shares (equivalent to approximately 2 million ADSs) and the remaining amount under the current share repurchase program is $19.1 billion as of September 30, 2025.

When combining the different factors, we can be optimistic and assume a bottom-line growth in the double-digits for the years to come.

Alibaba: Capable to Invest Heavily

Alibaba will continue to invest heavily, and management is expecting that the bottom line will continue to fluctuate due to these investments. In the earnings release, Toby Xu, CFO, made the following statement.

Our core businesses continued to deliver solid revenue growth, with AI revenue contributing to an expanding share of our cloud revenues from external customers, and customer management revenue up 10%. We are re-investing our profits and free cash flow for the future while near-term profitability is expected to fluctuate. Over the past four quarters, we have deployed approximately RMB120 billion in capital expenditure toward AI and cloud infrastructure

While Alibaba will use a huge part of its free cash flow to invest in the next few quarters, we should not ignore the still great balance sheet. On September 30, 2025, Alibaba still had RMB 135.1 billion in cash and cash equivalents and RMB 193.2 billion in short-term investments.

Alibaba also has RMB 26.3 billion in short-term borrowings on its balance sheet as well as RMB 255.3 billion in long-term debt (including bank borrowings, unsecured senior notes and exchangeable bonds). However, cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments are still enough to repay all of the company’s outstanding debt. Therefore, Alibaba still has a great balance sheet, and we should not be too worried about the company’s heavy spending so far.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

When looking at the two simple valuation multiples – the price-earnings ratio as well as the price-free-cash-flow ratio – we see Alibaba trading for 21.6 times earnings as well as 20.2 times free cash flow. These are valuation multiples that are neither cheap nor expensive. And especially in case of the price-free-cash-flow ratio, Alibaba is trading for much higher valuation multiples than in the last few years.

For a very long time, Alibaba was trading for an extremely low valuation multiple and when comparing the current P/FCF ratio to the last few years, Alibaba seems to be expensive. And while it is easy to call a business undervalued, that is still growing and trading for single-digit valuation multiples, we have to take a close look when a stock is trading for a valuation multiple of 20 as such a stock can be undervalued (in case of high expected growth rates) as well as overvalued (if the business is rather stagnating).

Hence, it is a much better way to determine an intrinsic value by using a discount cash flow calculation. As always, we are calculating with a discount rate of 10 % (the annual return I like to achieve at least) and the last reported number of diluted shares outstanding (19,168 million shares, which is resulting in 2,396 million shares for the ADR).

Now we must answer the question what free cash flow might be reasonable for Alibaba in the years to come. The free cash flow of the last four quarters was $17.94 billion, and I often use this number as the basis in our calculation. However, in case of Alibaba we should be very cautious. On the one hand, I would argue that free cash flow might be lower in the next few quarters. In my last article I already argued that due to heavy investments and high capital expenditures, the free cash flow will be rather zero or maybe even negative in the coming quarters.

And while the free cash flow might be even lower in the next few quarters, we can assume that the free cash flow will be much higher in the future. Alibaba is currently trading for a free cash flow margin of 12.8% and this is close to the lowest free cash flow margin ever. On average, Alibaba reported a free cash flow margin of 33.7% since its IPO.

Let’s be cautious and assume $0 free cash flow for the next two years due to heavy investments. For the next year we assume a free cash flow margin of 20% again (which is still rather cautious) and when also assuming that revenue will be around $165 billion then, we get a free cash flow around $33 billion. For the years to come, let’s be cautious and assume 6% growth (for the years following 2028 as well as till perpetuity). Calculating with these assumptions we get an intrinsic value of $206.86 for Alibaba and the stock would still be undervalued.

In my opinion, we can be even more optimistic and assume not only a higher free cash flow margin, but also slightly higher growth rates for the next few years. Once again, we assume $0 free cash flow for the next two years, and then we assume a free cash flow margin of 25% resulting in a free cash flow of $41.25 billion. We also assume a growth rate of 8% in the years to come followed by 6% growth till perpetuity in 10 years from now. This is resulting in an intrinsic value of $289.75.

Conclusion

In my opinion, Alibaba remains one of the better investments for the long run. The company is investing heavily in AI and it is a very diversified business that might profit in several ways from the AI-revolution. And while Alibaba might be able to grow at a high pace (high single digits or even double digits) for several years, the stock is still trading for a more reasonable valuation multiple than several U.S. AI companies.

Of course, this is not the best argument to make, but we should not ignore that Alibaba is still trading about 50% below its all-time high – indicating at least that sentiment was once a lot more bullish. And I see the previous all-time high certainly as a reasonable target within the next one to three years as Alibaba should be able to grow the stock price at a high pace and certainly outperform the market.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: EWZS: Brazilian Small-Caps Still Look Appealing Despite New Macro Challenges
data.description:
Opportunity Overview

Brazil's economy faces weaker growth and other economic risks this year, yet small-cap equities appear attractively priced in the market. While issues like inflation and rising debt levels have impacted Brazil, it has emerged victorious in other high-risk areas, such as the trade war tensions of 2025.

Brazilian equities have been one of the main drivers of emerging market returns in 2025.

Brazil still looks like an excellent hold amid the economic setbacks ahead in 2026 and 2027. The country's Central Bank has arguably been overcautious with rates, relative to Latam peers, and it will be in a better position to cut rates to support growth in 2026. Inflation has been tame, and the economy is poised to benefit from any increase in certain commodity prices, such as oil, iron, or agricultural products, due to its export structure.

The iShares MSCI Brazil Small-Cap ETF (EWZS) is a solid, attractively priced investment in the market, even after its stellar run in 2025. Investors should hold this position and accumulate during any potential dips in 2026.

Brazil's Economic Outlook

Brazil's economy has improved massively since 2022, most notably in combating inflation, and one of the main challenges it faces now is slower growth leading into 2026.

Weak Growth in Recent Quarters: Brazil's Finance Ministry recently lowered its growth forecasts for 2025 to 2.2% following weaker performance in Q2 2025. This growth was at the weakest levels experienced in the past five years, and this trend may continue in subsequent quarters.

Brazil's GDP Annual Growth Rate

Growth Will Decline Next Year: Santander Research projects that Brazil's GDP growth will fall below 2% in 2026 and 2027, which will be well below its post-COVID average. Other measures, like its new VAT tax reform, could help support growth. However, the economy will also need other supporting factors, such as trade stability and lower interest rates, to bring growth back to its historical average.

Brazil Has Been Cautious With Interest Rates: Although inflation has improved substantially, the country's Central Bank has taken a cautious approach to its monetary policy, which will result in slower growth. Brazil's Central Bank chose to keep its benchmark interest rate unchanged at 15% last November.

Brazil's Benchmark Interest Rate

This move is extremely cautious relative to some of its regional peers, which have kept rates much lower despite similar inflationary risks.

Country May 2024 Benchmark Interest Rate Current Benchmark Interest Rate
Brazil 10.5% 15%
Colombia 11.75% 9.25%
Chile 6% 4.75%
Peru 5.75% 4.25%
Mexico 11% 7.25%

Inflation Improvements: The improvement in inflation has been broad-based, as energy prices have dropped, and grocery prices only rose by 4.5% YoY in October. Inflation should remain moderate in 2026, slightly above its previous pre-Covid lows.

Diversified Commodities Growth: Brazil is poised to benefit from any positive commodity price movements, particularly in areas like oil, soybeans, and iron, which account for 5.1% of GDP but 14.3% of its exports.

Soaring Sovereign Debt: One common issue that has affected all emerging markets, including regional peers, has been the increase in sovereign debt in emerging markets. Brazil has improved substantially since 2020, but still has higher debt levels relative to peers like Chile.

Trade War Risks: Brazil is also vulnerable to trade war tensions, as the United States is a major trading partner, yet its relationship with China has been very fruitful, allowing it to offset weaker performance in the US market.

Brazil's exports rose by nearly 2% during the first ten months of this year, despite the 30+% plunge in exports to the United States.

Unique Small Cap Approach

Small-cap stocks in Brazil appear very attractively positioned at the moment, but may be vulnerable to short-term pullbacks in the coming quarters due to weaker macro data from Brazil and a general correction in emerging market equities.

Small Caps Have Underperformed: Brazilian small-cap stocks performed exceptionally well in 2025, following decades of massive underperformance. Other EM small-cap stocks have outperformed Brazil since 2011.

Appeals of this ETF: The iShares MSCI Brazil Small Cap ETF is an ideal vehicle to maintain exposure to Brazil, mainly because of its low valuation. The ETF currently trades at 9.5x earnings and 1.06x book value. The ETF also has an attractive composition relative to other Latam ETFs, as it is overweight consumers and underweight financials, a unique trait among emerging market ETFs.

Other ETFs: If you want to gain exposure to Brazil, some of your main options include other general Brazil-focused ETFs, which primarily focus on mid-caps and large caps. While this ETF outperformed its peers in 2025, it has been a long-term underperformer, historically more vulnerable to macroeconomic setbacks in Brazil.

Brazil's Relative Performance: Brazilian equities have outperformed peers like Chile and Colombia, and have been outperformed by Peru and Mexico. While Brazilian equities have been in the middle in terms of performance in the past decade, small-cap stocks have been massive underperformers, underperforming the peer average by over 60 percentage points.

The outperformance of EM equities in 2025 is a new trend, which may not continue under weakening economic conditions in 2026.

Risks and Final Thoughts

As economic risks in Brazil increase, it may be more ideal to hold onto larger ETFs that focus on mid-cap and large-cap stocks instead. These ETFs have outperformed strongly since May 2022, when inflation began to pick up globally.

Brazil's economy will still face a lot of risks in 2026. Its growth will likely be slower, despite VAT reforms and its strong positioning to cut interest rates, which could cause equity markets to sell off in 2026. The country's public debt levels are also above those in other regional markets.

Emerging markets have also had a very stellar run and could be due for a pullback on the back of any macro or geopolitical risks. While markets like Brazil have been able to lean on other major trade partners like China in 2025, trade war shocks could have a spillover effect on economic and equity market performance in 2026. Moreover, the US and Brazil have been having open dialogues recently and have reduced tariffs in some areas.

The iShares MSCI Brazil Small Cap ETF is an absolute gem based on valuation, and definitely looks like a safe hold at the moment. However, other larger ETFs like the iShares MSCI Brazil ETF could be safer bets, as they have historically shown their ability to have more stable performance amid economic shocks.

data.title: PFIX: Sell By Decoupling
data.description:
The Simplify Interest Rate Hedge ETF (PFIX) is a market option that acts as macroeconomic insurance against long-term interest rates. The main objective of this ETF is to protect an investment portfolio (1) against possible rises in long-term interest rates (20-30 years) and (2) against episodes of high volatility in the fixed income markets, while also seeking to provide a small carry to the portfolio through the purchase of US Treasury securities. It is actively managed and uses derivatives to achieve the above objective.

From my point of view, the big move with PFIX has already been made in the past two years. If we enter a cycle of cuts (as this situation seems to indicate) and normalization of the term premium, the ETF will most likely cease to provide useful convexity to a converter's portfolio and become an expensive insurance policy with negative carry, eroding the returns of that portfolio over time. In this context, it makes more sense to me to sell or reduce the position rather than maintain it as a structural position.

To achieve the aforementioned objective, the ETF implements the following strategy. The management team typically divides the fund's assets into two roughly equal blocks, consisting of (1) interest rate derivatives and (2) income-generating debt instruments (carry); these may be regular Treasury bonds, TIPS, or other ETFs.

In the derivatives block, the most commonly used are swaptions, which are options on interest rate swaps. When purchasing payer swaptions, these instruments give the fund the right to pay a fixed interest rate and receive a floating rate in the future. This type of position is sensitive to rate increases, since if long-term fixed rates rise, the right to pay the old (lower) rate and receive the new (higher) rate becomes very valuable, and the swaption appreciates, thus achieving part of its return.

The second leg is through the purchase of bonds or other instruments such as ETFs. These serve as collateral for the derivatives strategy and in turn provide carry, with the coupons associated with them.

ETF Overview

Analyzing the ETF profile using the image above, we can see the following relevant information. The fund currently manages a portfolio of USD 186 million, with 3.15 million shares outstanding, trading at a moderate discount to NAV of 1.6%. The 30-day SEC yield is around 3-3.5%, confirming that the bond/ETF leg provides some monthly income, but that the core of its return is in fact in the derivatives block mentioned above.

This second snapshot focuses on the fund's expenses and fees. PFIX currently charges a management fee of 0.50% and does not apply any 12b-1 fees or other additional expenses, so the total expense ratio remains at 0.50%. For a product that uses OTC swaptions and a complex overlay on the long end of the curve, this fee is relatively low when compared to other tail-hedging vehicles, where fees can easily reach 1–2% per annum.

The Portfolio

With the above image in hand, which shows the current position of the fund, we can say that we are dealing with an ETF exposed to sovereign risk and interest rate risk. Virtually 100% of the fund's exposure is in US government debt, which in turn serves as a hedge against the derivatives used by the ETF itself in its strategies. We can see that it does so because its position is greater than 100% (111%), meaning that it is somewhat leveraged to government exposure via Treasury bonds.

In the second image, we analyze the fund's current top 10 holdings. As can be seen, the core of the portfolio consists of treasury bills and a money market ETF (Simplify Government Money Market), which acts as its liquidity buffer. There are also a few lines labeled as Swaptions with double-digit weights, which are what really define the fund's performance, explaining why the total of the top 10 exceeds 140% of the NAV.

Payoff Profile

By studying the fund's historical performance, we can see that since its launch, the ETF has achieved a return of around 80-90%, with an APR of 15%. Although this may be misleading, this figure has been driven by episodes of stress in interest rates, as can be seen in the return for the last year (1.80%). The fund does not generate stable annual returns; rather, it has periods of flat or poor returns, and it is when interest rate shocks occur that the fund obtains its gains via derivatives.

The Premia

The image above shows when the ETF trades at a discount to its NAV and when it trades at a premium. It can be seen that most of the time it trades at a discount, as it is an illiquid vehicle with a relatively regular natural flow of sales. Only when the market seeks hedging against long-term rates do conditions arise where the vehicle trades at a premium to its NAV. I mention this so that readers bear in mind that they should try to anticipate entries, looking for moments when the ETF trades at a discount to its NAV, even though I am now recommending selling the ETF.

Final Thoughts

To conclude the article, we include in our analysis the breakdown of the yield on 10-year US bonds and future interest rate expectations (metric 2). The first metric indicates that the repricing period is now over, which is why we say that PFIX's big moment already happened in 2021-2023, when bond yields skyrocketed due to increased rate expectations and the term premium. If we look at the end of the first chart, we see that the opposite is currently happening, with a flattening of rate expectations and a relatively contained term premium. If this continues, the PFIX ETF will cease to be a source of returns and become an expensive policy that reduces your portfolio's performance. Furthermore, if we look at the evolution of interest rate expectations (metric 2), we see that the market is already discounting a cycle of cuts as the central scenario, which means that investors who remain long in PFIX today are, in practice, betting against the consensus of the implied curve, while obtaining a negative carry. That is why I recommend selling in this case. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Equities Are Expensive, Debt Is Cheap: I'm Buying Debt Hand Over Fist
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

We are coming off one of the deepest bear markets in history. This might sound odd to you, given that the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq are near all-time highs. An inherent bias among investors today is the tendency to think of everything in terms of common equities.

Common equities have done so well, for so long, that many investors simply assume that the proper way to invest is to invest all their money into common equities. It is a strategy that has been very effective for about 15 years or so.

We aren't talking about the equity markets; we are talking about the bond markets. Debt prices declined dramatically in 2022 and bottomed in 2023. They remain lower than they were 5 years ago:

Treasuries, municipals, MBS, and corporate bonds are all trading at lower prices today.

It is a fantastic buying opportunity, especially when you juxtapose the low prices of debt investments with the high prices of equities.

Many investors are intimidated by the process of buying bonds directly in their portfolio. Today, we're going to look at two investments that will do the investing for you.

Let's dive in!

Pick #1: DMB – Yield 4.8%

BNY Mellon Municipal Bond Infrastructure Fund (DMB) is a CEF (Closed-End Fund) that invests in municipal bonds. Municipal bonds are interest-rate sensitive because they have very long terms. As a result, municipal bond funds saw declining NAVs, and most of them reduced their dividends during the Fed's aggressive rate-hiking cycle. Now, we are on the other side of the cycle. Interest rates are moderating, NAVs are recovering, and dividends are coming back up.

When we wrote about DMB in October, we concluded:

"In terms of the distribution, DMB has taken a more cautious approach relative to many peers and is distributing only 3.85% of NAV, while many peers are distributing more. We saw DMB raise its distribution this year, and we expect we will see another raise next year as the Fed resumes its cutting cycle.

DMB has price upside from a rising NAV and a large discount that can shrink; it has ample room to raise its distribution, and its distributions are federal tax-exempt."

That week, DMB increased its dividend 10% to $0.042/month—that was the second dividend increase in 2025 and a 40% increase over the dividend in November 2024.

Going forward, DMB's NAV will benefit from lower long-term rates. With a leveraged duration of 13.31 years, we can expect every 100 bps in interest rate declines to increase NAV by approximately 13%.

The high duration is driven by a 60% allocation to loans that have maturities more than 20 years from now: Source

Additionally, higher-rated bonds tend to be more interest-rate-sensitive. Over 56% of DMB's portfolio is rated A or higher, and over 76% is investment grade:

For the dividend, Fed rate cuts will help the dividend grow faster. DMB is a leveraged fund, which means that as the Fed cuts, its cost of borrowing declines. Meanwhile, the bonds it owns are fixed-rate. So, as the Fed's target rate comes down, DMB is experiencing higher cash flows. Many of DMB's peers have opted to raise their dividends and are paying out +7% yields. DMB has decided to remain conservative and keep the dividend consistent with current cash flow. This means that it is able to reinvest 100% of the principal repaid and doesn't need to rely on selling bonds to fund the dividend.

While it is a bit lower yield than our other funds, DMB provides an attractive discount to NAV (about 10% today), strong potential for NAV growth, and a growing distribution.

Pick #2: AGNC – Yield 13.8%

AGNC Investment Corp. (AGNC) is a mortgage REIT that specializes in agency MBS (Mortgage-Backed Securities). Agency MBS are the driving force of mortgages in the United States. The "agencies" were created by the government for the purpose of making mortgages more accessible for the average American. They achieve this by removing the credit risk for investors. The agencies Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac buy up mortgages that meet their underwriting requirements, put a guarantee on them, and then sell them packaged as MBS to investors.

This helps originators because they can originate loans knowing that there is a definite buyer. To offset the risk of mortgage rates changing between when the loan is approved and when the sale closes, the originator can short the agency MBS market by selling future contracts. When a buyer "locks in" their mortgage rate, this is what the originator is doing. As a result, an originator doesn't experience capital constraints in their ability to originate as many qualifying mortgages as they can.

It helps the borrowers by creating uniform and clear underwriting requirements. It ensures that borrowers who qualify can easily get mortgages and that those mortgages are at a competitive rate. Non-Qualifying Mortgages are usually higher-interest, even when the borrower has a superior credit profile.

This brings us to the investors, including AGNC. Investors gain the knowledge that if the underlying borrower defaults, the agency will buy back the mortgage at par, making the investor whole. By taking on the credit risk, the agencies make agency MBS an attractive alternative to US Treasuries. Agency MBS is frequently used as collateral by banks or insurance companies in the overnight "repo" markets to meet their cash needs. Repurchase agreements are available at rates that are just a small premium over the Federal Reserve's Target Rate because the collateral is considered low risk.

AGNC uses a lot of leverage, primarily with repurchase agreements, and was leveraged at 7.6x debt/equity last quarter. AGNC's earnings are primarily a result of the average yield on the MBS they hold minus their average cost of funds: Source

Currently, that is 4.95% as their average yield, minus 3.17% for their cost of funds, for a spread of 1.78%. AGNC has seen its earnings decline over the past two years primarily due to its cost of funds rising faster than its average asset yield. This is because AGNC had interest rate swaps that were taken out in 2021 when interest rates were really low. Those swaps are maturing, and their cost of funds has approached the current market rate.

The current rate for 5-7 year swaps is 3.2-3.3%: Source

So, there is less than 25 bps difference between AGNC's cost of funds last quarter and the swaps they can enter into today. Meanwhile, the current coupon MBS is paying 5.0%. This means that the spread AGNC experienced last quarter is the current spread they can get on new investments today. Both their average asset yield and cost of funds are close to leveling out.

This is why we believe that the decline in AGNC's earnings is at an end. If the Fed continues its cutting cycle, AGNC will see its earnings start to climb as it can enter into cheaper swaps. If the Fed cuts aggressively in 2026, we could see the Target Rate go below 3.25%, which would allow AGNC to borrow cheaper outright.

Currently, the outlook in the Fed Futures market is about a 46% probability that the Fed's Target Rate will be 3% or lower by the end of 2026: Source

That is certainly a long way out, and a lot can change, but historically we have seen interest rates go much lower. AGNC is at a point where it's paying a very good dividend now, and we are perfectly content owning it if the dividend stays flat. The potential for AGNC to increase its dividend in 2026 or 2027 is a cherry on top.

Conclusion

Today, we looked at a CEF that invests in municipal bonds. It cut its dividend and saw a declining NAV during the bond bear market. Today, it is in recovery and is raising its dividend back up as interest rates stabilize.

The other takes a more aggressive approach to debt investing—buying agency MBS and using a significant amount of leverage. This provides a higher dividend and volatility that is a bit more equity-like. AGNC's use of interest rate swaps allowed it to maintain its dividend over the past 5 years, but the price bottomed in October 2023. It isn't a coincidence that the various debt ETFs all bottomed in the same month.

Debt is recovering from a bear market, while equities are rallying to all-time highs in their bull market. While many investors are eager to pay premium prices for equities, debt investments and debt-related investments have been a key focus for us for the past several years—we have funds like DMB and AGNC, we have a robust portfolio of preferred equity, and we have a bond ladder.

Buy debt while it is still at a low price.
